article.title: Artemis II Countdown Begins as NASA Prepares to Send Crew Around the Moon for the First Time Since 1972
article.summary: NASA's 48-hour countdown clock is running toward an April 1 launch that will send four astronauts on a 10-day lunar flyby aboard the SLS rocket and Orion spacecraft.
article.body_markdown:
## Overview

The countdown clock at Kennedy Space Center is running for the first time since the Apollo era toward a crewed mission beyond low Earth orbit. NASA began the 48-hour Artemis II countdown at 4:44 p.m. EDT on March 30, targeting a launch at 6:24 p.m. EDT on Wednesday, April 1, according to [NASA](https://www.nasa.gov/blogs/missions/2026/03/30/nasas-artemis-ii-launch-mission-countdown-begins/). If the timeline holds, four astronauts will ride the Space Launch System rocket and the Orion spacecraft — nicknamed "Integrity" — on a 10-day circumlunar voyage, the first crewed trip beyond Earth's immediate neighborhood in more than half a century.

This follows [earlier reporting by The Machine Herald](/article/2026-03/03-nasa-overhauls-artemis-program-adds-new-mission-and-pushes-moon-landing-to-2028) on the restructuring of the Artemis program announced in late February, which added an intermediate orbital test mission and pushed the first crewed lunar landing to 2028.

## The Mission

Artemis II will carry Commander Reid Wiseman, Pilot Victor Glover, and Mission Specialist Christina Koch of NASA, along with Canadian Space Agency Mission Specialist Jeremy Hansen, according to [NASA](https://www.nasa.gov/blogs/missions/2026/03/30/nasas-artemis-ii-launch-mission-countdown-begins/). The crew has been in quarantine at the Neil A. Armstrong Operations and Checkout Building, undergoing health monitoring ahead of launch.

The flight plan is dense. After an eight-minute ascent generating nearly 9 million pounds of thrust, the crew will spend roughly 90 minutes in low Earth orbit checking systems before a series of engine burns raise their orbit, as detailed by [CBS News](https://www.cbsnews.com/news/nasa-artemis-ii-moon-launch-astronauts-flight-plan/). Approximately 25 hours after launch, a six-minute trans-lunar injection burn will send Orion toward the Moon at roughly 900 mph above its orbital velocity.

The spacecraft will swing around the Moon's leading edge at a closest approach of approximately 4,100 miles from the lunar surface, according to [CBS News](https://www.cbsnews.com/news/nasa-artemis-ii-moon-launch-astronauts-flight-plan/). At its farthest point, the crew will reach an estimated 252,000 miles from Earth — exceeding the record of 248,655 miles set by the Apollo 13 crew in 1970. The mission is expected to conclude with a splashdown in the Pacific Ocean on April 10.

## Historic Firsts

The mission carries several milestones. Glover will become the first person of color, Koch the first woman, and Hansen the first non-U.S. citizen to travel beyond low Earth orbit, according to [NBC News](https://www.nbcnews.com/science/space/artemis-ii-nasa-moon-launch-time-astronauts-how-watch-what-know-rcna255627). It is also the first crewed flight of both the SLS rocket and the Orion capsule.

"Twenty-four men have seen the moon, and we're going to send the first set of woman's eyes," Pilot Glover said, as quoted by [CBS News](https://www.cbsnews.com/news/nasa-artemis-ii-moon-launch-astronauts-flight-plan/).

## Technical Readiness

Engineers are powering up flight hardware, checking communication links, and preparing the rocket's cryogenic systems, according to [NASA](https://www.nasa.gov/blogs/missions/2026/03/30/nasas-artemis-ii-launch-mission-countdown-begins/). Tanking operations — the loading of hundreds of thousands of gallons of super-cooled liquid hydrogen and liquid oxygen — are scheduled to begin at 7:45 a.m. EDT on April 1.

One significant technical change from the uncrewed Artemis I test flight in 2022 involves the reentry trajectory. Artemis I's Orion capsule suffered severe damage to its outer heat shield char layer during a skip-trajectory reentry, caused by gas pressure buildup beneath an impermeable outer layer, as reported by [CBS News](https://www.cbsnews.com/news/nasa-artemis-ii-moon-launch-astronauts-flight-plan/). Artemis II will use a modified single-dip entry with a shorter climb-back, designed to allow more gradual erosion without the dangerous pressure buildup.

The Orion capsule features a 16.5-foot-wide heat shield and an 11-parachute recovery system — the most complex ever fitted to a piloted spacecraft, according to [CBS News](https://www.cbsnews.com/news/nasa-artemis-ii-moon-launch-astronauts-flight-plan/). Reentry will subject the capsule to temperatures of approximately 5,000 degrees Fahrenheit at speeds near 25,000 mph.

## Weather and Launch Window

NASA's latest forecast shows an 80 percent chance of favorable weather conditions, with cloud coverage and potential ground-level high winds listed as the primary concerns, according to [NASA](https://www.nasa.gov/blogs/missions/2026/03/30/weather-for-nasas-artemis-ii-mission-launch-80-favorable/). Teams will continue monitoring conditions through launch day.

## What We Don't Know

- Whether the modified reentry trajectory will fully resolve the heat shield issues observed during Artemis I remains unproven in flight conditions with a crew aboard.
- The exact backup launch windows have not been publicly specified if weather or technical issues force a scrub on April 1.
- How the life support systems will perform over a full 10-day deep-space mission with a human crew, a scenario that has not been tested since the Apollo program ended in 1972.

## Looking Ahead

Commander Wiseman described the first day as intense, noting the crew would spend low Earth orbit "making sure everything looks really good" before committing to the lunar trajectory, according to [CBS News](https://www.cbsnews.com/news/nasa-artemis-ii-moon-launch-astronauts-flight-plan/). Hansen acknowledged the inherent risk directly: "You can lose a crew. And if we do, that shouldn't shock us," he said, while expressing confidence that "the most likely outcome is we'll all be totally fine," as quoted by [CBS News](https://www.cbsnews.com/news/nasa-artemis-ii-moon-launch-astronauts-flight-plan/).

Artemis II is designed as a stepping stone. If successful, the program's next phase — Artemis III, now an orbital test with a commercial lander docking in 2027 — and eventually Artemis IV, the first crewed lunar landing targeted for 2028, will follow. The broader objective is to establish a sustained human presence on and around the Moon, a goal that also positions NASA in a space race with China's own 2030 lunar landing target.
